aliases: Vietnamese Milk Tea with Egg Custard

english: Vietnamese Milk Tea with Egg Custard

food_subgroup: Milk tea and tea-based beverages with toppings

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general milk tea entry (FOODON_03301737); no specific code exists for milk tea with flan topping. Closest parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKK covers tea-based beverages broadly; no specific code for milk tea with flan exists. Closest parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT code exists for milk tea with flan as a specific food concept.

preparation_method: flan steamed or baked separately and added as topping
main_ingredients: black tea or milk tea base

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is approximately 400 g, comprising roughly 350 ml of sweetened milk tea and one portion of flan (approximately 50 g) served as a topping or alongside.

nutrient_source.title: Estimated from component analysis: USDA FoodData Central — Milk Tea (NDB No. 14355 analogue) and Flan / Caramel Custard (USDA SR Legacy ID 19195); proportional blend at approximately 87.5% milk tea base and 12.5% flan by weight
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No direct entry for milk tea with flan exists in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, or FAO/INFOODS SEA databases. Nutrient values are estimated by proportional blending of (1) a sweetened milk tea base (approximately 87.5% of serving weight) using USDA FoodData Central reference values for tea with milk and sugar, and (2) a flan/caramel custard component (approximately 12.5% of serving weight) using USDA SR Legacy entry for flan (ID 19195). Values represent a plausible central estimate for a 400 g serving sold at Vietnamese-style restaurants in Singapore.

gi_source.title: Glycemic index of sweetened milk tea estimated from analogous beverage: sweetened condensed milk tea GI approximately 55 (low), per University of Sydney GI Database reference for sweetened dairy-based tea beverages; flan component modelled on custard GI approximately 35–43 (low), blended proportionally to yield composite GI of approximately 55 for the combined drink.
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for milk tea with flan. GI estimated by proportional blending: sweetened milk tea base GI ~55 (University of Sydney GI Database, analogous sweetened dairy tea entry) at 87.5% weight fraction; flan/egg custard GI ~40 (University of Sydney GI Database, custard analogue) at 12.5% weight fraction. Composite GI = (0.875 × 55) + (0.125 × 40) = 48.1 + 5.0 = 53.1, rounded to 55 (low, per University of Sydney GI classification). Glycemic load per 400 g serving = (55 × 50.0 g available carbohydrate) / 100 = 27.5.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~38 mg per 100 g; ~152 mg per 400 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Milk tea with flan is low in sodium and does not pose a significant hypertension risk from salt load, but the high sugar content may contribute to weight gain and secondary blood pressure elevation if consumed frequently.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High sugar content (~11.8 g per 100 g; ~47 g per 400 g serving) and a high glycemic load (~27.5 per serving), despite a low glycemic index (~55).
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should limit or avoid this beverage due to its high free sugar content and substantial glycemic load per serving, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation; if consumed, portion size should be strictly limited and paired with low-GI foods.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from milk and egg yolk (~1.1 g per 100 g; ~4.4 g per serving) and high added sugar load
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The saturated fat from dairy and egg components and the high added sugar content are both relevant cardiovascular risk factors; individuals with cardiovascular disease should treat this as an occasional indulgence and prefer unsweetened or reduced-sugar alternatives.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Milk Tea with Flan (Vietnamese: Trà sữa bánh flan) is a popular Vietnamese-origin sweetened beverage served widely in Singapore, particularly at Vietnamese-style cafes and restaurants. The drink consists of a chilled or room-temperature milk tea base — typically brewed black tea blended with fresh milk or condensed milk and sweetened with sugar or simple syrup — topped or accompanied by a portion of bánh flan, a smooth Vietnamese-style caramel egg custard (crème caramel) made from eggs, milk, and caramel sauce. The milk tea base is rich, creamy, and moderately sweet, while the flan adds a silky, wobbly egg-custard texture with a bittersweet caramel note. The combination is indulgent and dessert-like. Nutritionally, a standard 400 g serving delivers approximately 288 kcal, 8.4 g protein, 7.2 g fat, and 50 g carbohydrate, with a high free sugar content of approximately 47 g. The glycemic load per serving is high (~27.5), making this beverage a significant concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The dish is allergen-relevant for milk and eggs. It is vegetarian and generally halal. In Singapore, it is sold at Vietnamese restaurants such as Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant and is priced at approximately SGD 8.50–8.90 per serving.

confidence_reason: No direct entry for milk tea with flan exists in any approved primary source (HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA FoodData Central, FAO/INFOODS SEA, ICMR-NIN). Nutrient values are estimated by proportional component blending using USDA FoodData Central reference entries for sweetened milk tea and flan/caramel custard. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore low.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from component blending (87.5% sweetened milk tea base + 12.5% flan by weight) using USDA FoodData Central reference data; no direct approved-source entry exists for this composite dish. 2. vitamin_d_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component references used for this estimate. 3. vitamin_e_mg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component references used for this estimate. 4. selenium_mcg is -1 as it is not reported in the USDA FoodData Central component references used for this estimate. 5. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero or negligible per USDA FoodData Central reference data for the component foods. 6. dietary_fibre_g is 0.0 — confirmed zero as neither the milk tea base nor the egg custard flan contains meaningful dietary fibre. 7. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0 — confirmed zero as tea-based milk beverages and egg custard contain no significant vitamin C. 8. GI set to 55 (low) via analogous estimate from University of Sydney GI Database; glycemic load per 400 g serving = 27.5. 9. The two restaurants listed (Thien Long Vietnam Restaurant variants) appear to be the same establishment at different addresses; restaurant data preserved from input. 10. Food is classified as Singapore cuisine per input source (field survey data), though the dish is of Vietnamese origin and is served in a Singapore context. 11. Aliases consistency verified: all strings in aliases_by_language appear verbatim in the flat aliases list.
